original_abstract:
Hydroponics represents a fundamental shift in agricultural methodology, enabling plants to thrive through mineral-rich water solutions entirely independent of soil. This ability to artificially introduce critical nutrients has paved the way for new efficiencies in cultivation. Parallel to such structural shifts in agriculture, the international labor landscape is experiencing a significant resurgence of the World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU). Long considered a relic of the past, the WFTU has re-emerged as a major force, particularly in South Africa. Within the Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU)—a primary affiliate of the International Trade Union Confederation (ITUC)—four member unions have already joined the WFTU, sparking a heated debate regarding the future of global labor unity.

This movement gained momentum in February 2012 when the WFTU Presidential Council convened in Johannesburg. Addressing the council, COSATU President Sdumo Dlamini signaled a willingness to re-evaluate the organization's international alliances, suggesting a potential bridge between the ITUC and the WFTU. This tension was palpable at the 11th National COSATU Congress, where the general secretaries of both rival federations, Sharan Burrow (ITUC) and George Mavrikos (WFTU), delivered high-profile speeches. Faced with shifting loyalties, Burrow adopted a diplomatic stance, emphasizing pluralism and freedom of association while downplaying ideological rifts involving socialism or communism. However, a significant rift remains. While Burrow expressed a desire for global worker unity, she identified a "fundamental difference" between the organizations, centered specifically on their diverging responses to the Arab Spring and the ongoing political revolutions in Syria and beyond. This paper explores whether these developments signal a permanent fracturing of the international trade union movement or a necessary evolution toward a more pluralistic labor landscape.
humanized_abstract:
This issue of New World Solidarity deals with two apparently distant topics. In the first article, it is discussed how hydroponics can become a new method of growing plants through solutions that have a higher concentration of minerals than in soil, thus increasing the productivity of the sector. At the same time, globally the World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU) is undergoing a tremendous revival and it has also made a great stride in South Africa. Four affiliates of the Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU) – the largest confederation in the ITUC – have already affiliated in the WFTU, triggering a heated debate within the ranks of labour internationalism regarding the prospects for a united labor movement worldwide.

In February 2012 the Presidential Council of the WFTU (World Federation of Trade Unions) gathered in Johannesburg, South Africa. During the meeting the President of COSATU (Congress of South African Trade Unions), Sdumo Dlamini, declared his willingness to reassess the unions in the international framework mentioning a possible bridge between the ITUC and the WFTU. During the 11th National COSATU Congress, the General Secretary of the ITUC, Sharan Burrow, and the General Secretary of the WFTU, George Mavrikos, made important speeches, as the union leaders are trying to manage the fragile balance between the two organisations. However, it seems that the break is irreversible. Burrow spoke about the “pluralism and the freedom of choice” avoiding to enter into ideological debates about socialism or communism, and admitting a “fundamental difference” with the WFTU regarding the way each organisation deals with the events of the Arab Spring and with the current wave of revolution in Syria and other countries worldwide. The question remains, whether the recent developments are a symptom of a split in the international trade union movement, which appears to be irreversible, or an opportunity for the rebirth of the labour movement, in a pluralistic and more dynamic way.
